issues.description: The change replaces `.unwrap_or(text)` with `.unwrap()`, directly contradicting the preceding comment 'Strip outer quotes safely (error recovery can produce malformed nodes)'. Tree-sitter's error recovery can absolutely produce string_literal nodes that do not start/end with `"` (e.g. unterminated strings at EOF, or a leading quote with an ERROR child). With `unwrap()`, any such input will panic the entire parser/LSP/compiler instead of producing a diagnostic. This is a robustness regression that turns recoverable parse errors into crashes.
issues.suggested_fix:
Restore the safe fallback. Either keep the previous `unwrap_or(text)` behavior, or — if you want noisier failure per the project's silent-defaults policy — return `None` (which the caller already handles) and emit a diagnostic, e.g.:

```rust
let s = text.strip_prefix('"').and_then(|s| s.strip_suffix('"'))?.to_string();
```

issues.description: The new `lower_single_quoted_string` uses `.unwrap()` on `strip_prefix('\'').and_then(strip_suffix('\''))`. Same panic hazard as above: tree-sitter error recovery can hand us a malformed node (unterminated literal, single leading quote, etc.) and the parser will abort instead of reporting a parse error. Because this function returns `Option<Expr>`, the natural fix is `?` so the caller can treat it as a parse failure.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace `.unwrap()` with `?` so malformed nodes yield `None` rather than panic: `let s = text.strip_prefix('\'').and_then(|s| s.strip_suffix('\''))?.to_string();`

issues.description: `lower_single_quoted_string` is not referenced anywhere in the crate (grep for the symbol returns no callers). No dispatch in `lower_expr` (or wherever string nodes are handled) was updated in this diff, so single-quoted string literals in the grammar will never reach this function. Either wire it into the node-kind match, or drop it until the grammar actually needs it — shipping unreferenced parser code invites drift and hides the panic risk above.
issues.suggested_fix: Add the corresponding match arm in the node-kind dispatch (e.g. `"single_quoted_string" => self.lower_single_quoted_string(node)`), and add a unit test exercising it. If the grammar doesn't yet produce such a node, delete the function and land it with the grammar change.

issues.description: `lower_string_literal` and `lower_single_quoted_string` are byte-for-byte identical except for the quote character. This is exactly the kind of copy-paste that later causes one copy to get fixed (e.g. escape handling) while the other rots.
issues.suggested_fix: Factor out a helper: `fn lower_quoted_string(&self, node, quote: char) -> Option<Expr>` and have both call sites go through it. Even better, accept the node and dispatch on its kind inside a single function.

issues.description: No tests accompany this change. The two behaviors that matter most here are untested: (a) a malformed/error-recovered string literal node must not panic (regression test for the `unwrap` change), and (b) a single-quoted string round-trips to `ExprKind::StringLiteral` with quotes stripped. Without these, the panic regression above would not be caught by CI.
issues.suggested_fix: Add parser tests covering: an unterminated `"foo` input, an unterminated `'foo` input, a well-formed `'hello'`, and assert no panic + expected `StringLiteral("hello")` / parse-error diagnostic.

issues.description: Single-quoted literals in many languages denote a distinct concept (char literal, raw string, symbol, etc.) from double-quoted strings. Collapsing both into `ExprKind::StringLiteral` with no escape-processing and no marker to distinguish them may be wrong depending on what the Reify grammar intends. This is worth confirming before landing — if the grammar added single-quoted strings as a synonym for double-quoted, fine; if they are meant to be char literals or raw strings, this silently drops that distinction.
issues.suggested_fix: Confirm grammar intent with whoever added the `single_quoted_string` node kind. If the semantics differ, introduce a new `ExprKind` variant (or flag) rather than aliasing onto `StringLiteral`. Also note neither function currently handles escape sequences (`\n`, `\'`, `\\`) — if the grammar allows escapes, both literals are broken.

issues.description: `lower_single_quoted_string` returns `Option<Expr>` but — as written after the `unwrap` — can only return `Some(...)` or panic. Either it should actually use `None` as the failure mode (see blocking item above) or the signature should be `Expr`. Mixed contracts across sibling lower_* functions make failure-mode reasoning harder.
issues.suggested_fix: Use `?` on the strip chain so the function honors its `Option` return type on malformed input.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The diff introduces two blocking robustness regressions: both `lower_string_literal` and the new `lower_single_quoted_string` replaced safe quote-stripping with `.unwrap()`, which will panic the parser on any malformed node produced by tree-sitter's error recovery — directly contradicting the existing safety comment and violating Reify's noisy-but-not-crashing failure policy. Additionally, `lower_single_quoted_string` is dead code (no callers, no dispatch wiring), duplicates `lower_string_literal` verbatim, ships with no tests, performs no escape handling, and may be semantically wrong if single-quoted literals are meant to be a distinct kind. Fix the `unwrap`s (use `?` and let the `Option` return handle error recovery), wire the new function into the node-kind dispatch (or delete it), deduplicate via a shared helper, and add regression tests for malformed and well-formed inputs before landing.
